results.question: What is the definition of an automated message system under the Electronic Transactions Act?

output.answer: Under the Electronic Transactions Act, an 'automated message system' is defined as "a computer program or an electronic or other automated means used to initiate an action or respond to data messages or performances in whole or in part, without review or" **[LAW-1]**.

results.question: How is 'land' defined under the Registration of Documents Ordinance?

output.answer: Under the Registration of Documents Ordinance, the term 'land' is defined to include "things attached to the earth or permanently fastened to anything attached to" the earth, unless the context otherwise requires **[LAW-5]**.

results.question: Draft a formal written complaint to the Consumer Affairs Authority on behalf of a consumer who purchased a defective refrigerator, citing the relevant provisions for the Authority to inquire into the matter.

output.answer:
# FORMAL COMPLAINT TO THE CONSUMER AFFAIRS AUTHORITY

## 1. CAPTION
**BEFORE THE CONSUMER AFFAIRS AUTHORITY OF SRI LANKA**

**In the matter of a complaint under the Consumer Affairs Authority Act No. 09 of 2003**

**Complainant:** `[CONSUMER NAME]`
**Respondent:** `[TRADER/MANUFACTURER NAME]`

## 2. TITLE OF THE PLEADING
**WRITTEN COMPLAINT REGARDING BREACH OF IMPLIED WARRANTY AND SUPPLY OF DEFECTIVE GOODS**

## 3. PARTIES
- **Complainant:** `[CONSUMER NAME]`, residing at `[ADDRESS]`, holder of NIC No. `[NIC NUMBER]`.
- **Respondent:** `[TRADER/MANUFACTURER NAME]`, carrying on business at `[BUSINESS ADDRESS]`.

## 4. JURISDICTION
This Authority is vested with the power to inquire into complaints regarding the breach of implied warranties and the supply of goods not conforming to standards under **[LAW-3]** and **[LAW-4]** of the Consumer Affairs Authority Act No. 09 of 2003.

## 5. MATERIAL FACTS
1. On `[DATE OF PURCHASE]`, the Complainant purchased a refrigerator (Model: `[MODEL NUMBER]`) from the Respondent for the sum of `[AMOUNT]`.
2. The Complainant expressly made known to the Respondent that the refrigerator was required for the purpose of domestic food preservation.
3. Upon installation, the refrigerator failed to function as intended, specifically `[DESCRIBE DEFECT, E.G., FAILURE TO COOL]`, rendering it unfit for the purpose for which it was supplied.
4. The goods supplied do not conform to the implied warranty of fitness for purpose as provided under **[LAW-3]**.

## 6. CAUSE OF ACTION
1. The Respondent has breached the implied warranty that the goods supplied would be reasonably fit for the purpose for which they were supplied, pursuant to **[LAW-3]**.
2. The goods supplied do not conform to the standards or specifications expected, nor to the implied warranty of quality, giving rise to a right of complaint under **[LAW-4]**.

## 7. RELIEF / PRAYER
Wherefore, the Complainant respectfully requests that the Authority:
1. Conduct an inquiry into this complaint as provided for under **[LAW-3]** and **[LAW-4]**.
2. Order the Respondent to replace the defective refrigerator or, in the alternative, refund the full purchase price of `[AMOUNT]` paid by the Complainant.
3. Order the payment of such compensation as the Authority deems fit for the inconvenience caused.

## 8. VERIFICATION
I, `[CONSUMER NAME]`, do hereby verify that the facts stated above are true and accurate to the best of my knowledge.

Date: `[DATE]`
Place: `[LOCATION]`

__________________________
Signature of Complainant

## 9. SCHEDULE
- Copy of Purchase Receipt/Invoice dated `[DATE]`
- Warranty Card (if applicable)
- Photographs of the defect (if available)

## 10. SIGNATURE
`[SIGNATURE]`
`[NAME OF ATTORNEY-AT-LAW (IF APPLICABLE)]`
`[ENROLLMENT NUMBER]`

## Notes for Completion
- Ensure the complaint is filed within one month of the supply of the goods as required by **[LAW-3]**.
- Attach copies of all relevant receipts and correspondence with the trader.
- If represented by an attorney, include their details in Section 10; otherwise, the Complainant should sign personally.

- ( d ) the  goods  supplied  will  be  reasonably  fit  for  the purpose for which they are supplied.

(2) Where a trader or any person other than a trader supplies any goods or provides any service to a consumer in the course of a business and the consumer, expressly or by implication, makes known to the trader or other person of any particular purpose for which the goods or services are required or the result that he desire the service to achieve, there is an implied warranty that the services provided under the  contract  for  the  provision  of  such  services  and  any materials supplied in connection with those services will be reasonably fit for that purpose or are of such a nature and quality that they might reasonably be expected to achieve that result, except where circumstances show the consumer does not rely, or that it is unreasonable for him to rely, on the trader's or such other person's skill or judgement.

(3) A consumer aggrieved by the breach of an implied warranty as provided for in subsection (1) or (2) may make a complaint to the Authority in writing against such breach within one month of the supply of such goods or the provision of such services as the case may be, or the supply of materials supplied in connection with the provision of those services.

(4) At any inquiry held into a complaint made under subsection (3), the Authority shall give the trader or other person against whom the complaint is made, an opportunity of being heard either in person or by an agent on his behalf.

(5) Where after the inquiry the Authority is of opinion that a breach of an implied warranty has taken place, it shall order the trader or other person to pay compensation to the aggrieved party or refund the amount paid for the supply of such goods or provision of such services as the case may be, and for the supply of any materials in connection with the provision of those services, within such period as shall be specified in the order.

results.question: Draft a boilerplate clause for a commercial supply agreement that explicitly recognizes the legal validity of electronic signatures and contracts formed via automated message systems, in accordance with the Electronic Transactions Act.

output.answer:
# Commercial Supply Agreement

## 1. Parties
This Agreement is entered into on this `[DATE]` by and between:

`[PARTY A NAME]`, having its registered office at `[ADDRESS A]` (hereinafter referred to as the "Supplier"); and

`[PARTY B NAME]`, having its registered office at `[ADDRESS B]` (hereinafter referred to as the "Purchaser").

## 2. Recitals
WHEREAS, the Supplier is engaged in the business of `[NATURE OF BUSINESS]` and the Purchaser desires to procure `[GOODS/SERVICES]` from the Supplier; and

WHEREAS, the parties intend to formalize their commercial relationship through this Agreement, including the use of electronic means for contract formation and execution.

## 3. Definitions
- "Electronic Record" shall have the same meaning as defined under the Electronic Transactions Act No. 19 of 2006 (as amended).
- "Automated Message System" refers to a computer program or an electronic or other automated means used to initiate an action or respond to data messages or performances in whole or in part, without review or intervention by a natural person.

## 4. Operative Clauses
4.1. The parties agree that this Agreement and any subsequent purchase orders may be executed in electronic form. In accordance with **[LAW-1]** and **[LAW-4]**, this Agreement shall not be denied legal validity or enforceability solely on the ground that it is in electronic form.

4.2. The parties acknowledge that a contract formed by the interaction of an automated message system and a natural person, or by the interaction of automated message systems, shall not be denied validity or enforceability solely on the ground that there was no review or intervention by a natural person **[LAW-2]**.

## 5. Representations and Warranties
Each party represents that it has the legal authority to enter into this Agreement and that any electronic communication or signature used in the performance of this Agreement shall be binding upon the respective party.

## 6. Termination
This Agreement may be terminated by either party upon `[NUMBER]` days' written notice in the event of a material breach by the other party. Termination shall not affect the validity of electronic records created prior to the date of termination.

## 7. Dispute Resolution
This Agreement shall be governed by and construed in accordance with the laws of the Democratic Socialist Republic of Sri Lanka. Any disputes arising out of this Agreement shall be subject to the exclusive jurisdiction of the courts of `[CITY/DISTRICT]`.

## 8. General Provisions
8.1. **Electronic Signatures:** Where this Agreement or any related document requires a signature, the requirement shall be deemed met if a method is used to identify the signatory and indicate their intention in respect of the information contained in the electronic communication, provided such method is as reliable as appropriate for the purpose **[LAW-5]**.

8.2. **Entire Agreement:** This document constitutes the entire agreement between the parties.

## 9. Execution

**For and on behalf of `[PARTY A NAME]`**

__________________________
Name: `[NAME]`
Designation: `[DESIGNATION]`
Date: `[DATE]`

**For and on behalf of `[PARTY B NAME]`**

__________________________
Name: `[NAME]`
Designation: `[DESIGNATION]`
Date: `[DATE]`

**Witnesses:**
1. _____________________
2. _____________________

## Notes for Completion
- Please insert the specific names, addresses, and dates in Section 1.
- Define the specific goods or services in Section 2.
- Specify the notice period for termination in Section 6.
- Insert the appropriate jurisdiction (e.g., Colombo District Court) in Section 7.
- Ensure that the electronic signature method chosen by the parties meets the reliability standards set out in **[LAW-5]**.

results.question: Kamal verbally agrees to sell his commercial property to Nimal for Rs. 50 Million. Nimal pays a Rs. 5 Million advance, and they shake hands on the deal in front of several witnesses. A month later, Kamal changes his mind, refuses to transfer the property, and refuses to return the advance. Nimal wishes to sue Kamal to force the sale of the property. Based on the formal requirements of the law, what legal hurdles does Nimal face in enforcing this contract?

output.answer:
## Issue

Whether a verbal agreement for the sale of commercial property, supported by an advance payment and witnesses, is legally enforceable to compel the transfer of said property under Sri Lankan law.

## Rule

Under Section 2 of the Prevention of Frauds Ordinance, No. 7 of 1840, certain classes of contracts—specifically those involving the transfer of land or interest in land—are subject to strict formal requirements:

> "Section 2 of the Prevention of Frauds Ordinance, No. 7 of 1840 declares that the following classes of contracts shall be of no force or avail in law unless in writing and signed by two witnesses in the presence of a Notary and duly attested by him." **[LAW-3]**

Furthermore, judicial precedent confirms that an agreement to transfer property requires a notarially attested writing to be binding **[LAW-4]**.

## Application

In the present scenario, Kamal and Nimal entered into a verbal agreement for the sale of commercial property. Nimal's attempt to enforce the sale faces a significant legal hurdle: the contract lacks the mandatory formal requirements prescribed by the Prevention of Frauds Ordinance. 

1. **Lack of Writing**: The agreement was verbal, whereas the law requires such contracts to be in writing **[LAW-3]**.
2. **Lack of Notarial Attestation**: The law mandates that the agreement be signed by two witnesses in the presence of a Notary and duly attested by said Notary **[LAW-3]**. The presence of witnesses at the handshake does not satisfy the statutory requirement of notarial attestation.
3. **Enforceability**: Because the contract fails to meet these statutory formalities, it is considered to be of "no force or avail in law" **[LAW-3]**. Consequently, Nimal cannot rely on the existence of the verbal agreement or the payment of the advance to compel Kamal to transfer the property, as the court cannot enforce a contract that does not comply with the Prevention of Frauds Ordinance **[LAW-4]**.

## Conclusion

Nimal faces a high probability of failure in an action to force the sale of the property. Because the agreement for the sale of land was not executed in accordance with Section 2 of the Prevention of Frauds Ordinance, No. 7 of 1840, it is legally unenforceable. The court is unlikely to compel the transfer of the property regardless of the advance payment or the presence of witnesses. 

*Caveat: This analysis is limited to the enforceability of the contract to transfer the property; it does not address potential separate claims for the recovery of the Rs. 5 Million advance under the law of unjust enrichment or other restitutionary principles, as those specific provisions were not provided in the source documents.*

The word " movables " as used in the definition for "  goods " does not include anything more than corporeal movables, and the term goods " in section 59 of that Ordinance  means all tangible movable property, except money  and  in  certain  cases crops and  things  attached  to the  soil.

T HE  defendant,  who  had  bought  from  the  Government  the  rent of  the  arrack  farm  of  the  town and  district  of  Negombo  for 1902 and 1903, agreed verbally to sell to the plaintiff the said rent. The  plaintiff complained of a  breach of this agreement by the  defendant  and  claimed  damages.

The defendant took the objection-  that the agreement was not enforceable by  action, inasmuch  as  it  was a  contract for  the sale of  "  goods,"  and  there  was  no  note  o f -  memorandum  of  it  in  writing as required by sub-section 1 of section 4 of Ordinance No. 11 of  1896.

The  'District^ Judge over-ruled  this  objection.

*,

A  chose  in  action  was  expressly  excluded  from  what  was  comprehended  in  the  term  " goods  "  used  in  the  English  Sale  of  Goods Act,  and  the  District  Judge  was  therefore  wrong  in  applying  to  the present case  the law as to choses in  action under the English Act. The present case was governed by Ordinance  No. 11 of 1896, and  in  it " goods  "  were  said  to  include all "  movables.

Issue: Whether a verbal agreement for the sale of immovable property is legally enforceable for specific performance.

Rule: Under Section 2 of the Prevention of Frauds Ordinance, no promise, bargain, contract, or agreement for establishing any security, interest, or encumbrance affecting land or other immovable property shall be of force or avail in law unless it is in writing and signed by the party making it (or by a person lawfully authorized) in the presence of a licensed notary public and two or more credible witnesses, and duly attested.

Application: The agreement between Kamal and Nimal for the sale of the commercial property was entirely verbal. Despite the payment of an advance and the presence of witnesses, the strict formal requirement of a notarial execution was not met. Therefore, the contract is statutorily void and of no force or avail in law.

Conclusion: Nimal cannot legally enforce the sale of the property (specific performance) because the verbal contract violates the Prevention of Frauds Ordinance. (Note: While Nimal may have a separate quasi-contractual claim in unjust enrichment to recover the advance, the contract of sale itself is unenforceable.)

results.question: A consumer hires a logistics company to transport valuable fragile goods. The standard form contract provided by the company contains a clause stating: 'The company accepts absolute zero liability for any loss or damage to goods in transit, even if caused by the direct negligence of our employees.' The goods are destroyed because the driver negligently crashed the truck while texting. The company points to the contract clause to deny the claim. How should a court evaluate the validity of this exclusion clause?

output.answer:
## Issue

Whether a contract term in a standard form agreement that purports to exclude all liability for loss or damage caused by the negligence of a company's employees is legally valid when dealing with a consumer.

## Rule

Under the Unfair Contract Terms Act of 1997, a party cannot, by reference to any contract term, exclude or restrict liability for breach of contract or negligence when dealing as a consumer or on the other party's written standard terms of business, except in so far as the term satisfies the requirement of reasonableness **[LAW-2]**. 

Furthermore, a contract term that purports to impose an obligation on a person dealing as a consumer to indemnify another person for liability incurred for negligence or breach of contract is only valid if it satisfies the requirement of reasonableness **[LAW-2]**. 

Additionally, regarding guarantees for goods ordinarily supplied for private use, any term that purports to exclude or restrict liability for loss or damage resulting from the negligence of a person concerned in the manufacture or distribution of the goods shall be of no effect **[LAW-3]**, **[LAW-5]**.

## Application

In the present scenario, the logistics company is utilizing a standard form contract to exclude "absolute zero liability" for damage caused by the "direct negligence" of its employees. 

1. **Standard Terms and Consumer Status**: As the company is using written standard terms of business against a consumer, the exclusion clause is subject to the requirement of reasonableness under the Unfair Contract Terms Act **[LAW-2]**. 
2. **Negligence**: The law explicitly restricts the ability of a party to exclude liability for negligence. Any such term must satisfy the test of reasonableness to be valid **[LAW-2]**. 
3. **Reasonableness**: While the provided sources establish that such clauses are subject to the test of reasonableness, they do not provide an exhaustive list of factors to determine what constitutes "reasonable." However, a clause that attempts to exclude *all* liability (absolute zero) for the direct negligence of employees is highly likely to be scrutinized heavily by a court under this standard **[LAW-2]**.

## Conclusion

The exclusion clause is not automatically valid. Under the Unfair Contract Terms Act, the clause attempting to exclude liability for negligence is subject to the requirement of reasonableness **[LAW-2]**. If the court finds that the term fails the test of reasonableness, it will be of no effect. The company cannot rely on the clause to deny the claim if the court determines the exclusion is unreasonable. 

*Caveat: The provided sources do not define the specific criteria for the "test of reasonableness," which would be a matter for judicial determination based on the facts of the case.*

results.ground_truth:
Issue: Whether a standard contract term excluding liability for property damage caused by negligence is legally valid and enforceable against a consumer.

Rule: Under Section 3(2) of the Unfair Contract Terms Act, No. 26 of 1997, a person cannot by reference to any contract term or to a notice given to persons generally or to particular persons exclude or restrict his liability for negligence resulting in loss or damage (other than death or personal injury) except in so far as the term or notice satisfies the 'requirement of reasonableness'. 

Application: The logistics company is attempting to use a blanket exclusion clause in a standard form contract to avoid liability for property damage caused directly by their employee's negligence. The court will not automatically enforce this clause. Instead, the court must subject the clause to the 'requirement of reasonableness'. Given that it is a sweeping exemption in a standard form contract offered to a consumer, it places an unfair burden on the consumer and is highly likely to be deemed unreasonable.

Conclusion: The exclusion clause is not automatically void, but it will only be effective if it passes the reasonableness test. Under these facts, a court is highly likely to strike down the clause as unreasonable, thereby holding the logistics company liable for the negligently destroyed goods.

results.question: How does the Unfair Contract Terms Act differentiate between contract terms that exclude liability for negligence resulting in death or personal injury, versus those excluding liability for other types of loss or damage, and what legal standard must the latter satisfy?

output.answer:
## Executive Summary

The Unfair Contract Terms Act, No. 26 of 1997, imposes strict limitations on the ability of parties to exclude or restrict civil liability for negligence in the course of business **[LAW-1], [LAW-4]**. The Act distinguishes between liability for death or personal injury and liability for other forms of loss or damage **[LAW-4], [LAW-8]**. While the former is rendered entirely ineffective by statute, the latter is subject to a 'reasonableness' test to determine its validity **[LAW-1], [LAW-6]**.

## Findings

### Exclusion of Liability for Death or Personal Injury
Under Section 3(1) of the Unfair Contract Terms Act, any contract term or notice that purports to exclude or restrict a person's liability for death or personal injury resulting from negligence is of no effect **[LAW-4], [LAW-8]**. This provision applies to business liability and serves as an absolute prohibition against such exclusions, regardless of the circumstances or the agreement of the parties **[LAW-1], [LAW-4]**.

### Exclusion of Liability for Other Loss or Damage
For loss or damage other than death or personal injury resulting from negligence, the Act adopts a more flexible approach. Section 3(2) stipulates that a contract term or notice attempting to exclude or restrict such liability shall have effect only insofar as it satisfies the 'requirement of reasonableness' **[LAW-6], [LAW-8]**.

### Acceptance of Risk
It is important to note that under Section 3(3), a person's agreement to a contract term or their awareness of a notice that purports to exclude or restrict liability for negligence does not, in itself, constitute an acceptance of any risk by that person **[LAW-4], [LAW-6]**.

## Conclusion

The Unfair Contract Terms Act provides a bifurcated legal framework for negligence-based liability exclusions. Liability for death or personal injury cannot be excluded under any circumstances, rendering such clauses void. Conversely, exclusions for other types of loss or damage are not automatically void but are subject to the statutory requirement of reasonableness. The provided sources do not define the specific criteria or factors that constitute the 'requirement of reasonableness' beyond stating that it is the necessary standard for such terms to be effective.

results.ground_truth: Under Section 3(1) of the Unfair Contract Terms Act No. 26 of 1997, any contract term or notice that purports to exclude or restrict liability for death or personal injury resulting from negligence is absolutely void and of no effect. However, under Section 3(2), a contract term or notice purporting to exclude or restrict liability for any other loss or damage resulting from negligence is not automatically void. Instead, it will only have legal effect in so far as such term or notice satisfies the 'requirement of reasonableness'.

results.question: Under the Prevention of Frauds Ordinance (as amended by Act No. 4 of 2024), what are the formal execution requirements for a transfer deed of immovable property, and how does the law accommodate a transferee who is a corporate body unable to be present?

output.answer:
## Executive Summary

Under the Prevention of Frauds Ordinance, the transfer of immovable property requires a notarial conveyance to be valid in law [LAW-1]. Such instruments must be in writing, signed by the transferor in the presence of a licensed notary and two or more witnesses, and duly attested by them [LAW-2], [LAW-4]. While the provided sources discuss the general requirements for notarial execution, they do not contain information regarding amendments by Act No. 4 of 2024 or specific procedures for corporate bodies unable to be physically present, other than noting that certain entities (like the National Savings Bank) may utilize a common seal in the presence of authorized members as an alternative to standard notarial attestation [LAW-5].

## Findings

### Formal Execution Requirements for Immovable Property

Under the Prevention of Frauds Ordinance, any sale, transfer, or assignment of immovable property must be executed in writing to be of force or avail in law [LAW-2], [LAW-4]. The mandatory requirements for such a deed are:

* **Written Instrument:** The transaction must be recorded in a written document [LAW-2].
* **Signature:** The deed must be signed by the party making the transfer [LAW-2].
* **Presence of Witnesses and Notary:** The execution must take place in the presence of a licensed notary and two or more witnesses who are present at the same time [LAW-2], [LAW-4].
* **Attestation:** The execution must be duly attested by the notary and the witnesses [LAW-2], [LAW-4].

Failure to comply with these requirements renders the instrument of no force or avail in law [LAW-2], [LAW-4]. Furthermore, a valid notarial instrument is a prerequisite for the transfer of title, though actual delivery of the deed is also required to constitute constructive delivery of the land [LAW-4].

### Execution by Corporate Bodies

The provided sources do not detail a general procedure for corporate bodies unable to be physically present. However, they highlight that specific statutory bodies may have unique provisions. For instance, the National Savings Bank Act allows for the transfer of property via a certificate of sale signed by the Bank, which is an exception to the general notarial requirement [LAW-1], [LAW-5]. In such cases, the Bank is required to affix its common seal in the presence of two members of the Board of Directors, who must sign the instrument to attest to their presence [LAW-5].

## Conclusion

The general rule for the transfer of immovable property in Sri Lanka is the requirement of a notarial conveyance executed before a notary and two witnesses [LAW-1], [LAW-2]. The provided documentation does not contain information regarding the specific amendments introduced by Act No. 4 of 2024, nor does it provide a general framework for corporate entities unable to be physically present, beyond the specific statutory exception provided for the National Savings Bank [LAW-5].

It  is  to  be  noted  that  section  55  of the  National  Savings  Bank  Act contain express provisions for the passing of title to immovable property on  a  certificate  of  sale  signed  by  the  Bank.  The  title  thus  bestowed by  the  certificate  of  sale  becomes  valid  by  operation  of  law.  It  would be  seen  that  the  requirement  in  section  55  of  the  National  Savings Bank  Act  by  expressly  providing  for  passing  of  title  on  a  certificate of  sale  without  a  notarial  attestation  directly  conflicts  with  section  2 of the Prevention of Frauds Ordinance. Therefore,  it is vital to consider whether these competing considerations effectively harm the objectives of  section  2  of  the  Prevention  of  Frauds  Ordinance.  Insofar  as  this aspect  is  concerned  it  is  noteworthy  that  effectiveness  and  sanctity leading  to  the  finality  of  the  certificate  of  sale  is  secured  by  section 3  of  National  Savings  Bank  Act  with  the  stipulation  to  affix  the  seal of  the  Bank  (which  is  in  the  custody  of  the  Board  of  Directors)  in the  presence  of  two  members  of  the  Board  who  are  mandated  to sign  the  instrument  in  token  of  their  presence.

Finally,  it  is  necessary  to  consider  whether  the  National  Savings Bank  Act  being  a  later  specified  enactment  takes  precedence  over the provisions of the Prevention of Frauds Ordinance by the application of  the  principle  ' generalia  specialibus  non  derogant ' in  the  interpre­ tation  of  statutes.  It  would  be  clear  that  upon  an  examination  of  the provisions  of  section  55  of  the  National  Savings  Bank  Act,  that  the legislature has recognized disposition of immovable property otherwise than  by  conveyance  exec'uted  before  a  Notary.

Ratnayake has ceased to practice after 27.11.194 and that he was not a notary public licensed to prac­ tice  as  at  19.7.1985,  the  date  on  which  the  disputed  deed  is said to  have been executed.

Evidence  of  Kumarihamy  clerk  of  the  High  Court  had  clearly established that the  last licence issued to  L.B.  Ratnayake was in the year  1984  and  after  1984  L.B.  Ratnayake  has  not  made  any  appli­ cation for a licence nor has he been issued any licence to practice as a  notary.

The  learned  District Judge  had  in  his judgment erroneously  stat­ ed  that  the  letters  that  had  been  written  by  Suraj  Ratnayake  have been sent,  at or about the  time the dispute  has  arisen  regarding the execution  of the  deed  in question.

It  is  to  be  observed  that the correspondence with the  Registrar of Lands  has  started  from  letter  V7  dated  22.03.1985  regarding  not sending  the  monthly  list  for  February  1985  which  was  long  prior  to the  start  of  the  dispute  regarding  deed  2007  (3)  with  the  complaint made  by  the  plaitniff-appellant  to  the  Kandy  police  on  30.08.1985 (V18)  regarding  the  purported  execution  of the  deed  in  question  on 19.07.1985.

It  is  of  significance  to  note  that  in  the  light  of  the  evidence  with regard to the last licence that had been issued to L.B.

7 .  No  contract  entered  into  in  Sri  Lanka  for  the  hire  and  service  of  any servant or journeyman artificer for any period of time longer than one month shall be valid in law, so as to subject any party thereto to the provisions of this Ordinance for not performing the same, unless such contract shall be in writing and shall clearly express the terms and conditions thereof, and shall be  signed  or  acknowledged  by  the  parties  thereto  in  the  presence  of  a Magistrate, or a Justice of the Peace, or other person expressly authorized by the Minister in charge of the subject of Justice, such Justice or other person not being himself the employer of such servant or journeyman artificer or the agent of such employer. And it shall be the duty of such Magistrate, Justice of the  Peace,  or  other  authorized  person  to  see  that  the  contract  is  fully explained  to  the  parties,  and  to  certify  on  the  contract  that  they  fully understand  the  terms  thereof  and  are  desirous  to  fulfil  the  same.  And  such contract,  when  produced  in  evidence  and  bearing  the  certificate  of  the Magistrate, Justice of the Peace, or duly authorized person as aforesaid shall be  prima  facie  evidence  of  the  matters  and  things  contained  therein.  And every such contract shall be executed in triplicate ; and it shall be the duty of such  Magistrate  or  Justice  of  the  Peace,  or  other  authorized  person  as aforesaid, to give or to cause to be given one copy thereof to the servant, and to  send  or  to  cause  to  be  sent,  within  ten  days  of  the  execution  thereof, another copy thereof to the Magistrate of the district wherein such contract shall  have  been  executed,  and  in  default  thereof  such  Magistrate  or  Justice shall be liable to a penalty of fifty rupees.

The  petitioners'  case is,  that  the petitioners,  Mr.  Pethiyagoda and the  Ceylon  Oils  &  Fats  Corporation  belong  to  a  class  of  similarly '  situated  persons,  namely.  Municipal  Tenants  in  the  same  Public ·Market  Complex;  the  by-laws  have  been  applied  in  an  unequal manner, favouring one party and not favouring the other party; there has  been  a  selective  enforcement  of  the  by-laws  against  the petitioners and a non-enforcement of the same against the other two and  this  constituted  a  violation  of  the  fundamental  right  of  equal protection  of  the  law,  guaranteed  to  them  by  Article  12(1)  of  the

Constitution.

The  petitioners  appeared  before  us  in  person  to  support  their application.  The  1st  petitioner  spoke for  himself and  on  behalf of his

wife as well.

The  1  st petitioner  is not a party to the tenancy agreements or to the tenancy relating to the two bare  lands.  In fact,  the tender conditions stipulate  that  tenderers  should  be  citizens  of  Sri  Lanka.  There  is  no legal  nexus  between  him  and  the  Kandy  Municipal  Council.  He, therefore, cannot complain of a breach of his fundamental right under

Article  12(1).

Two questions arise for our consideration:

- (1) Is  there  an  infringement  of  the  fundamental  right  of  the  2nd petitioner under Article  12(1) and
- (2)  Do the acts complained of constitute executive or administrative
3. action in terms of Articles  17 and  126 of the Constitution?

I   shall deal with the 2nd question first.

Learned attorney for the  1  st and  3rd  respondents,  who  raised this matter as an objection,  in limine,  submitted that having  regard  to the various  provisions  of the  Municipal  Council  Ordinance,  when  the two tests  laid  down  in Wijetunge  v. Insurance  Corporation  of  Ceylon (3) and Wijeratne  and  Another  v.  People's  Bank  and  Another (4) namely, the functional test and the governmental control test, are applied,  a  Municipal  Council  cannot  be  regarded'as  a  State  organ  or agency.

results.ground_truth: Under Section 2(1) and 2(2) of the Prevention of Frauds Ordinance (as amended by Act No. 4 of 2024), a transfer deed for immovable property must be in writing, signed by every executant and the witnesses in the presence of a licensed notary public, all present at the same time and in the presence of one another. Crucially, both the transferor and transferee must affix their signatures and thumb impressions. If the transferee is a corporate body and its Board of Directors is unable to be present to execute the deed, the Board may authorize in writing any other person to sign and affix their thumb impression on the Board's behalf, provided that person complies with the simultaneous presence and attestation requirements of subsection (1).
